ports.name: London
ports.note: Busiest port in the world by the late 18th c.

ports.name: Liverpool
ports.note: Rising Atlantic & dominant British slave-trade port.

ports.name: Amsterdam
ports.note: VOC/WIC entrepot; Europe's former financial hub.

ports.name: Bordeaux
ports.note: France's leading colonial/Atlantic port; wine.

ports.name: Nantes
ports.note: France's chief slave-trading port.

ports.name: Cadiz
ports.note: Carrera de Indias monopoly hub.

ports.name: Lisbon
ports.note: Brazil & Estado da India trade.

ports.name: Gothenburg
ports.note: Chartered 1621; before that the dot is era-named Älvsborg, the Göta estuary's harbour. Swedish East India Co. (SOIC); 132 documented expeditions.

ports.name: Kingston
ports.note: Great Caribbean sugar entrepot; Jamaica station. Jamaica's harbour from the English capture of 1655 — era-named Port Royal until the 1692 earthquake, Kingston after.

ports.name: Salvador da Bahia
ports.note: Direct South-Atlantic Brazil-Mina trade.

ports.name: Whydah (Ouidah)
ports.note: Major slave-embarkation port; the African side of the Atlantic trade.

ports.name: Batavia (Jakarta)
ports.note: VOC Asian headquarters from 1619; before that the dot is era-named Jayakarta (Sunda Kelapa), the same harbour — the junk and Bugis trades sail it all era.

ports.name: Canton (Guangzhou)
ports.note: Sole legal Western-trade port in Qing China.

ports.name: Tranquebar
ports.note: Danish Asiatic Co. factory, Coromandel coast; Fort Dansborg from 1620.

ports.name: Nagasaki (Dejima)
ports.note: The harbour and its factory island together: at roughly a kilometre apart they are one dot at chart scale, so the SEPARATION between Dejima (the Dutch factory) and Nagasaki (the port and its Chinese quarter) is carried in the flow matrix and the vessel's ledger rather than by two overlapping marks.

ports.name: Danzig (Gdańsk)
ports.note: The grain staple of the 'mother trade'; Hanseatic, under the Polish crown.

ports.name: Riga
ports.note: Masts and naval stores. Hanseatic → Swedish (1621) → Russian (1710); the Hanse flag smears this, noted.

ports.name: St Petersburg
ports.note: Peter's window, from 1703; lanes era-gated accordingly.

ports.name: Newcastle-upon-Tyne
ports.note: The coal trade — Europe's largest generator of ship movements, invisible to the foreign-going rankings by declared basis.

ports.name: Livorno
ports.note: The free port — every flag welcome, which was the business model.

ports.name: Boston
ports.note: Founded 1630. British colonial to 1783; later lanes fly the US flag.

ports.name: New York
ports.note: New Amsterdam from 1625; English New York from 1664.

ports.name: Philadelphia
ports.note: Founded 1682. Staged at the Delaware capes for the 1° grid.

ports.name: The Chesapeake
ports.note: The tobacco coast — Norfolk/Annapolis, staged at the capes.

ports.name: Louisbourg
ports.note: The Newfoundland Banks fishery harbour, era-named: St John's carries the cod trade before 1713 and after 1758; between, France's fortress Louisbourg — founded 1713, fallen and demolished after the siege of 1758. One node stands for the fishery coast; St John's proper lies ~500 km east (roster-scale staging).

ports.name: Portobelo
ports.note: Founded 1597. The isthmus fair; the galeones' terminus to 1739. Displayed lat nudged 9.6→10.0 (~45 km N) so it snaps to a Caribbean-side ocean cell now that the Panama seal is a lat-9 land wall (see bake-routes.mjs) rather than a lat-6..10 box — the wall keeps the Pacific port of Panama snapping to the Gulf of Panama on its own side.

ports.name: Bridgetown (Barbados)
ports.note: English settlement from 1628.

ports.name: Cap-Français
ports.note: Saint-Domingue's great port, founded c.1670; its lanes end with the revolution of 1791.

ports.name: St Eustatius
ports.note: The Golden Rock — the free-port contraband entrepôt. Dutch from 1636.

ports.name: Rio de Janeiro
ports.note: The gold fleets; the court itself after 1808.

ports.name: Luanda
ports.note: The largest single embarkation point of the Atlantic slave trade. Sober framing applies to every human-cargo lane.

ports.name: Elmina
ports.note: 'The mine' — Portuguese to 1637, then the WIC's Gold Coast castle.

ports.name: Saint-Louis (Senegal)
ports.note: French factory at the Senegal mouth from 1659.

ports.name: Surat
ports.note: India's greatest port for a century and a half — the Gujarati merchant fleet under the Mughal peace.

ports.name: Bombay
ports.note: English from 1661 (EIC 1668). Before that the dot is era-named Goa — the Carreira da Índia's counted terminus (the flow matrix's declared simProxy; Goa proper lies ~400 km south, roster-scale staging) — until Goa's own promotion.

ports.name: Madras
ports.note: Fort St George from 1639; before that the dot is era-named Masulipatnam, the Coromandel coast's dominant port — continuity proxy for a coast whose trade long predates the fort.

ports.name: Calcutta
ports.note: EIC settlement from 1690; before that the dot is era-named Hugli, Bengal's river port on the same Hooghly. Staged at the Hooghly mouth for the 1° grid.

ports.name: Mocha
ports.note: The coffee staple that supplied Europe before Java.

ports.name: Amoy (Xiamen)
ports.note: The Nanyang junk trade's great Fujian port — the private diaspora, not the Qing state.

ports.name: Manila
ports.note: Where American silver met Chinese silk.

ports.name: Banda Neira
ports.note: The world's only nutmeg source until the 1770s; the VOC monopoly dates from the conquest of 1621 — at its most absolute, and most brutal.

ports.name: Makassar
ports.note: Gowa's free-trade entrepôt to 1669; the Bugis network's hearth after.

ports.name: Naha (Ryukyu)
ports.note: The licensed tribute conduit between China and Japan.

ports.name: Istanbul
ports.note: The largest city of Europe and the Near East — present here because the flow matrix can carry what the rankings could not (the declared-boundary exemplar). Reached through the carved Bosporus.

ports.name: Kaffa (Feodosia)
ports.note: The Crimean Khanate's port; its trade as represented here ends with the Russian annexation of 1783. The slave trade is under the sober human-trafficking framing, never ordinary cargo.

ports.name: Arkhangelsk
ports.note: Russia's White Sea door, founded 1584 — reachable only in the summer/autumn ice season (the corridor is seasonal in the baked fields).

ports.name: Smeerenburg (Spitsbergen)
ports.note: 'Blubber town', c.1614–1660 — abandoned when the bay whaling gave out and the fishery moved offshore. The whaling season only; no winter field reaches it, which is the history.

ports.name: Sitka (Novo-Arkhangelsk)
ports.note: Russian America, from 1799 — sea-otter furs for the Canton market.

ports.name: Acapulco
ports.note: The Manila galleon's eastern terminus from 1571; the silver crosses to Veracruz overland, which is the point.

ports.name: Algiers
ports.note: The Regency's capital and the best-known corsair base; from 1830 the seat of French Algeria. Its trade in this chart is the Regency export flow, not the corsairing, which was predation rather than a trade lane and is in the silences register.

ports.name: Tunis
ports.note: The dot sits at La Goulette, the sea gate of Tunis proper, which lies inland of the lake. Ottoman from 1574, Husainid from 1705.

ports.name: Tripoli
ports.note: The Regency capital and the Mediterranean terminus of the Saharan caravan routes; Karamanli 1711-1835. The caravan traffic itself is overland and is registered as a silence, not sailed.

ports.name: Alexandria
ports.note: Ottoman Egypt's Mediterranean port and one of the Levant trade's principal echelles. Long carried in the flow matrix without a dot; sailable from this build.

ports.name: Scanderoon
ports.note: Iskenderun (Alexandretta), the sea gate of Aleppo and the Levant Company's chief unloading place. Its levant-trade lanes existed in the matrix but could not fold without a dot.

ports.name: Smyrna
ports.note: Izmir, the principal echelle of the Levant trade and by the 18th century the busiest of them. It had been folding onto Istanbul - about 330 km away, and the wrong kind of place: the empire's capital standing in for its chief commercial outlet.

ports.name: Callao
ports.note: The port of Lima and the Armada del Mar del Sur's base: Peruvian silver went north from here to Perico for the isthmus crossing and the Portobelo fair. Its fall in January 1826 was the last Spanish position in South America.

ports.name: Guayaquil
ports.note: The Armada's shipyard and, after the reopening of 1774, a cacao port booming into the nineteenth century.

ports.name: Panamá (Perico)
ports.note: The Pacific end of the isthmus. A lane endpoint rather than a trading node in its own right: the silver crossed to Portobelo overland, by mule, and that crossing is not modelled as shipping.

ports.name: Valparaíso
ports.note: Chile's Pacific outlet; after independence (1818) the first port of call for shipping round the Horn and a rising entrepôt through the 1830s and 40s.

ports.name: Nootka Sound
ports.note: The anchorage of the maritime fur trade, in Mowachaht territory: American and British ships watered and traded here for sea-otter pelts bound for Canton, and did so on Maquinna's terms. Active 1785-1841, when the trade ended with the otter rather than with any policy.

ports.name: Curaçao
ports.note: Willemstad: the Dutch West India Company's Caribbean entrepot and, under the asiento, a slave depot for re-export to the Spanish Main. It had been folding onto St Eustatius, roughly 700 km away - two quite different Dutch islands collapsed into one dot.

ports.name: St Thomas
ports.note: Danish from 1672, and thin until the free-port declaration of 1764 turned Charlotte Amalie into a neutral entrepot that traded through everyone else's wars.

ports.name: Paramaribo
ports.note: Suriname's capital, Dutch from 1667: a coffee and sugar plantation colony distinct from the sugar islands, and worked by people carried there under middle-passage.

ports.name: Belize
ports.note: The Bay of Honduras cutting settlement, English-speaking from the 1660s - deliberately not dated to the contested 1638. Logwood first, mahogany from the 1770s, cut by enslaved gangs far up the rivers.

ports.name: New Orleans
ports.note: Founded 1718; French, then Spanish from 1763, then French again and American with the Louisiana Purchase of 1803. The Mississippi's outlet and, by the 1830s and 40s, the great cotton port - and the largest slave market in the United States, whose coastwise traffic is carried soberly in neworleans-coastwise.

ports.name: Montevideo
ports.note: Founded 1724 to hold the Rio de la Plata against Portuguese Colonia; the estuary's deep-water port, and the Plata's Atlantic gate for hides, tallow, and salt beef.

ports.name: Mombasa
ports.note: A Swahili-coast port, Portuguese at Fort Jesus and then Omani from 1698; Mazrui governors held it semi-independently for much of the eighteenth century.

ports.name: Zanzibar
ports.note: The Omani sultanate's African seat - Sayyid Said moved his court here in 1840 - and the hub of the clove boom, the ivory caravans, and the East African slave trade that grew while the Atlantic one was suppressed.

ports.name: Ilha de Moçambique
ports.note: Portuguese from 1507: the Carreira's East African call and the administrative seat of Portuguese East Africa.

ports.name: Malé
ports.note: The Maldive sultanate's seat, and the source of the cowrie shells that were counted out as currency across the Bay of Bengal and, by way of Europe, on the West African coast.

ports.name: Port Louis (Île de France)
ports.note: The French Indian Ocean base from 1735 and the corsair harbour of the revolutionary wars; British from 1810, when it became Mauritius. Sugar and, later, indentured labour.

ports.name: Shanghai
ports.note: A great Yangtze-delta junk port long before the treaty of 1842 made it a foreign one: the coastal grain and cotton trade ran through here on a scale the European record barely registers.

ports.name: Tianjin (Dagu)
ports.note: The dot sits at Dagu, the Hai river bar. The northern terminus of the coastal grain fleets that fed Peking - a Chinese domestic trade, and one of the largest movements of shipping anywhere in the era.

ports.name: Busan (the Waegwan)
ports.note: The Japan House at Busan, where the Tsushima So handled all licensed Korea-Japan trade under Joseon supervision.

ports.name: Tsushima
ports.note: The So domain's island, the licensed intermediary of the Korea-Japan trade in both directions.

ports.name: Ayutthaya (the Chao Phraya bar)
ports.note: The Siamese capital lay some eighty km up the Chao Phraya; sea-going ships anchored at the bar and lightered up, so the dot sits at the river mouth rather than on the city. Sacked in 1767, which ends the window.

ports.name: Aceh
ports.note: The sultanate at Sumatra's northern tip: the pepper port that shipped direct to the Red Sea and Jedda, outflanking the Portuguese route round the Cape.

ports.name: Bantam
ports.note: Banten Lama, the pepper emporium of the archipelago and the first Dutch and English factory in Java - a separate place from Batavia, about eighty km away, and its rival for eighty years until the VOC took it in 1682-84.

ports.name: Ostend
ports.note: The Austrian Netherlands' Indies port: private voyages from 1715, the Ostend Company 1722-27, and a Bengal afterlife to 1744.

ports.name: Davis Strait
ports.note: Not a port but a whaling ground, between Greenland and Baffin Island: the bowhead fishery the Dutch, British, and Nantucket fleets worked from the 1720s. A grounds node, following Smeerenburg's precedent.

ports.name: Jedda
ports.note: The Hijaz port: Mecca's sea gate for the pilgrimage, and the Red Sea terminus of the pepper and spice trade that came direct from Aceh. It had been folding onto Mocha, about 800 km south and a different trade — coffee, not pepper and pilgrims.

ports.name: Singapore
ports.note: Founded 1819 and a free port from the start; within twenty years the entrepot of the Straits, drawing the archipelago's junk and prahu trade into a British harbour.

ports.name: Hong Kong
ports.note: Ceded in 1841 at the end of the first Opium War. A deep-water harbour with no hinterland, which is precisely what it was taken for.

ports.name: Sydney
ports.note: Founded 1788 as a penal colony; wool from the 1820s made it a trading port as well. The convict transportation that founded it is carried soberly in its own flow, not as cargo.

ports.name: Basra (the Shatt bar)
ports.note: The head of the Gulf and the Ottoman terminus of the Baghdad caravan route; the dot sits at the Shatt al-Arab bar, since Basra itself lies far up the river.

ports.name: Bandar Abbas
ports.note: Safavid Persia's Gulf outlet from 1622, when Abbas took Hormuz from the Portuguese with English help; silk out, and the Gulf's link to the Indian country trade.

ports.name: York Factory
ports.note: The Hudson's Bay Company's chief post on the Bay, from 1684: furs out, trade goods in, one or two ships a year through an ice-free window of a few weeks.

ports.name: The Pacific whaling grounds
ports.note: Not a port but the offshore grounds off Peru and Chile, opened by the Emilia in 1788 and worked by the British and then overwhelmingly American sperm fishery. A grounds node, like the Davis Strait and Smeerenburg.

powers.name: Great Britain
powers.note: Dominant naval and mercantile power across the era.

powers.name: France
powers.note: Britain's chief rival; Atlantic colonial and slaving trade.

powers.name: Dutch Republic
powers.note: Fades after 1795 (Batavian Republic / French client).

powers.name: Kingdom of the Netherlands
powers.note: Succeeds the Dutch Republic (which ends 1795). The Japan trade 1817-1858 ran on the Netherlands Indies colonial government's account from Batavia in chartered private hulls - NOT the NHM, which took over only in 1859 (verified 2026-07-18).

powers.name: Spain
powers.note: Carrera de Indias; frequently allied to France.

powers.name: Portugal
powers.note: Brazil & Estado da India; usually British-aligned.

powers.name: Sweden
powers.note: Neutral carrier; Baltic iron/timber and SOIC China trade.

powers.name: Denmark-Norway
powers.note: Neutral carrier; Danish Asiatic Co.

powers.name: United States
powers.note: Post-1783 only; neutral-trade carrier and China trade.

powers.name: East India Company
powers.note: British monopoly on the India & China trade.

powers.name: V.O.C. (Dutch East India Co.)
powers.note: Dissolved 1799; Batavia & Dejima trade.

powers.name: Swedish East India Co.
powers.note: Gothenburg-Canton tea trade.

powers.name: Danish Asiatic Co.
powers.note: Tranquebar & Canton trade under Danish neutral flag.

powers.name: Russia
powers.note: Diversity layer (PLAN-2 §5): Arkhangelsk furs & naval stores; the Russian-American Co. after 1799. Spawns once its ports are baked (Phase B).

powers.name: Ottoman Empire
powers.note: Diversity layer: Smyrna's Levant trade, Kaffa, Ottoman Yemen (Mocha). Carriage largely Greek- and Armenian-crewed under the Ottoman flag.

powers.name: Omani Empire
powers.note: Diversity layer: Muscat's dhow trade; took the Swahili coast from Portugal (Mombasa 1698).

powers.name: Safavid Persia
powers.note: Diversity layer: Bandar Abbas silk & cotton after Hormuz falls (1622); fades with the Safavid collapse.

powers.name: Ryukyu Kingdom
powers.note: Diversity layer: Naha's licensed tribute trade — the tiny kingdom that laundered the China–Japan exchange.

powers.name: Sultanate of Gowa
powers.note: Diversity layer: Makassar's free-trade spice smuggling against the VOC monopoly, until the city falls in 1669.

powers.name: Kingdom of Siam
powers.note: Diversity layer: Ayutthaya's crown junks in the Japan and China trade, until the city's destruction in 1767.

powers.name: Republic of Ragusa
powers.note: Diversity layer: the neutral Adriatic carrier — 'argosy' is her word — under Ottoman protection.

powers.name: Duchy of Courland
powers.note: Diversity layer: Duke Jacob's improbable colonial fleet out of Windau (Tobago, the Gambia).

powers.name: Brandenburg-Prussia
powers.note: Diversity layer: the Brandenburg African Company's brief Gold Coast venture out of Emden.

powers.name: The Hanse towns
powers.note: S2 backbone flag: Hamburg, Danzig, Riga — the Hanseatic and post-Hanseatic German-Baltic carriers. Riga's sovereignty changes (Swedish 1621, Russian 1710) are smeared under this flag, noted.

powers.name: Republic of Venice
powers.note: S2 backbone flag: the Serenissima, to its fall in 1797.

powers.name: Republic of Genoa
powers.note: S2 backbone flag: banker and carrier of Habsburg silver.

powers.name: Tuscany (Livorno)
powers.note: S2 backbone flag: the free port's own small marine; most Livorno traffic flies other flags, as it did. Extended to 1850 with the era: the Grand Duchy of Tuscany lasted until 1859, and Livorno's free port went on trading through the Napoleonic interruption and the 1815 restoration.

powers.name: Kingdom of Naples
powers.note: S2 backbone flag: the Mezzogiorno's grain and consumption trade.

powers.name: Mughal India (Gujarati marine)
powers.note: S2 flag: Surat's own merchant fleet — India's ships in India's trade (Das Gupta). Fades with Mughal authority after 1700; ends as a spawning flag c.1760.

powers.name: The Nanyang junk trade
powers.note: S2 flag (user-funded): the private Fujian/Canton junk diaspora — distinct from the Qing state (a non-spawning shore power). THE Euro-centrism corrective made sailable.

powers.name: Tsushima (Sō domain)
powers.note: S2 flag (user-funded): the licensed carrier of the Korea trade — ready for Busan's promotion (tranche 2). Era extended to 1850: the 1815 end was the old chart horizon. The So domain held its Korea trade to the Meiji restoration, and the Maldive sultanate ran to 1953.

powers.name: The Bugis network
powers.note: S2 flag: the archipelago's own carriers — Makassar under Gowa, then the diaspora after 1669 (Reid). Carries the asserted bugis-carrying system.

powers.name: Golconda (Qutb Shahi)
powers.note: S2 flag bundle: Masulipatnam's sovereign — non-spawning shore power, ready for the port's promotion (tranche 2).

powers.name: Sultanate of Patani
powers.note: S2 flag bundle: the queens of Patani — non-spawning shore power, ready for the port's promotion (tranche 3).

powers.name: W.I.C. (Dutch West India Co.)
powers.note: Diversity layer: the Atlantic counterpart of the VOC — Brazil, Elmina, St Eustatius, and the treasure-fleet war.

powers.name: Russian-American Company
powers.note: Diversity layer: Sitka sea-otter furs for the Canton market.

powers.name: African polities (embarkation)
powers.note: Not a spawning flag — the coastal polities (Hueda, later Dahomey) that controlled embarkation at Whydah. Present so the African side of the Atlantic trade is named, not effaced.

powers.name: Efik city-states
powers.note: Diversity layer, not a spawning flag: Old Calabar's merchant houses (Duke Town, Creek Town), who controlled the Bight of Biafra trade on their own terms.

powers.name: Joseon Korea
powers.note: Diversity layer, not a spawning flag: Busan's Japan House (Waegwan) — trade conducted by licensed Tsushima vessels. Era extended to 1850 with the chart: Joseon lasted to 1897 and the Qing to 1912 — the 1815 end was the old horizon, not history.

powers.name: Zamorin of Calicut
powers.note: Diversity layer, not a spawning flag: the Malabar pepper sovereign who resisted the Portuguese monopoly for two centuries.

powers.name: Maldive Sultanate
powers.note: Diversity layer, not a spawning flag: Malé's cowrie-money fishery, carried to Bengal in local and Indian bottoms. Era extended to 1850: the 1815 end was the old chart horizon. The So domain held its Korea trade to the Meiji restoration, and the Maldive sultanate ran to 1953.

powers.name: Crimean Khanate
powers.note: Diversity layer, not a spawning flag: Kaffa's Black Sea trade — including its slave market, to be treated with the same sober factual framing as the Atlantic trade.

powers.name: Nguyễn lords (Đàng Trong)
powers.note: Diversity layer, not a spawning flag: Hội An's licensed junk fair — silk, ceramics, aloeswood.

powers.name: Qing China
powers.note: Not a spawning flag — controls Canton, where Western company ships were confined to the Thirteen Factories. Era extended to 1850 with the chart: Joseon lasted to 1897 and the Qing to 1912 — the 1815 end was the old horizon, not history.

powers.name: Regency of Algiers
powers.note: The Regency of Algiers, conventionally dated from 1516 (NOT 'Ottoman from 1525'). Ends 1830 at the French conquest; the port itself continues under france from that year via eraPowers.

powers.name: Regency of Tunis
powers.note: The Regency of Tunis: Ottoman from 1574, Husainid from 1705, still a Regency past 1850.

powers.name: Regency of Tripoli
powers.note: The Regency of Tripoli: Ottoman from 1551, Karamanli 1711-1835, then Ottoman again. The maritime terminus of a Saharan caravan trade.

powers.name: Mowachaht (Nuu-chah-nulth)
powers.note: Nootka Sound was Mowachaht territory, and Maquinna's people controlled who traded there - the American and British fur ships anchored at their sufferance, not in a vacant harbour. A SHORE power (it spawns no vessels, like african and qing): the ships on the nootka-fur lanes fly American and British colours, which is the honest division. Flagging the anchorage itself American would have written the owners out of their own harbour.

powers.name: Peru
powers.note: Independent from 1821; Callao fell in January 1826, the last Spanish position in South America.

powers.name: Chile
powers.note: Independent from 1818. Valparaiso became the first Pacific call for shipping round the Horn and a rising entrepot through the 1830s and 40s.

powers.name: Austrian Netherlands (Imperial)
powers.note: The double-headed eagle at sea: private Ostend voyages from 1715, the chartered Ostend Company 1722-27, wound up by the Treaty of Vienna, with a Bengal afterlife at Banquibazar to 1744. Deliberately clamped to that span.

powers.name: Sultanate of Aceh
powers.note: The pepper sultanate at Sumatra's northern tip, shipping direct to the Red Sea and outflanking the Portuguese Cape route. Its own polity and its own shipping — it was filed under the Zamorin here by mistake, which put an Acehnese fleet under a Malabar flag.

powers.name: Sultanate of Banten
powers.note: Banten: the archipelago's pepper emporium and Batavia's rival until the VOC conquest of 1682-84. Its own sultanate, not Gowa's — the two are a thousand miles and a different people apart.

powers.name: Hudson's Bay Company
powers.note: Chartered 1670. One or two ships a year to the Bay and home again inside the ice-free window — a tiny, utterly regular traffic.

shipTypes.name: Ship of the line
shipTypes.note: Battle-fleet capital ship; rare at sea outside fleet actions.

shipTypes.name: Frigate
shipTypes.note: Cruiser / escort; the workhorse warship of the era.

shipTypes.name: Sloop-of-war
shipTypes.note: Small naval vessel; convoy escort and dispatch.

shipTypes.name: East Indiaman
shipTypes.note: Large armed company merchantman for the India & China trade; stands in for the earlier great carrack/galleon on the pre-1700 India & Brazil runs.

shipTypes.name: Fluyt
shipTypes.note: Dutch bulk carrier (from c.1595), famously small crew; declining after mid-century.

shipTypes.name: Merchantman
shipTypes.note: Generic full-rigged ocean trader; the workhorse stand-in for the ordinary 16th–17th-c merchant hull before the named rigs diverge.

shipTypes.name: Snow
shipTypes.note: Largest two-masted rig; coastal and mid-Atlantic trade.

shipTypes.name: Sloop
shipTypes.note: Small fast trader; Caribbean and coastal runs.

shipTypes.name: Slave ship (Guineaman)
shipTypes.note: Purpose-fitted for the Atlantic slave trade; abnormally large crew relative to tonnage for control of captives. Abolition is modelled per-flag at the LANE level, not on the type: British & US lanes end 1807, French lanes 1793, while the Portuguese Brazil-Mina lanes continue to 1815 (Brazil's trans-Atlantic trade persisted for decades after 1808).

shipTypes.name: Galleon
shipTypes.note: The great armed carrier of the Iberian age — Carrera flotas, Manila galleons, Armada capital ships. Fades as the frigate/Indiaman split emerges around 1700. Movement on the frigate polar (PLAN-2 §5.3).

shipTypes.name: Carrack (nau)
shipTypes.note: The nau of the Carreira da Índia and the Brazil sugar fleet — the largest hulls afloat in the 16th century. Movement on the indiaman polar.

shipTypes.name: Caravel
shipTypes.note: The nimble Iberian coaster-explorer, still working the Atlantic runs into the early 17th century. Movement on the brig polar.

shipTypes.name: Junk
shipTypes.note: Diversity layer (PLAN-2 §5.3): the East & Southeast Asian ocean trader — Ryukyu tribute ships, Siamese crown junks, the Hội An fair, the Amoy junks that made Singapore. Own parametric polar since S2 (battened lug: vmax 8.0 kn, no-go 56°) — literature-derived, uncalibrated (declared boundary). Era extended 1815→1850 at increment 6d: the junk trade ran the whole age of sail.

shipTypes.name: Dhow (baghlah)
shipTypes.note: Diversity layer: the Indian-Ocean and Arabian monsoon trader — Muscat, Mocha, the cowrie runs, the Bugis prahus at Singapore. Own parametric polar since S2 (lateen/settee: vmax 7.0 kn, no-go 50°) — literature-derived, uncalibrated (declared boundary). Era extended 1815→1850 at increment 6d: the dhow/prahu monsoon trades ran the whole era.

cargo.name: in ballast (empty)
cargo.note: No cargo; stone/sand for trim. Common on homeward or repositioning legs.

cargo.name: salt
cargo.note: Setúbal/Bay salt for the fisheries and Baltic.

cargo.name: coal
cargo.note: Tyne & Wear coal — the collier trade, Britain's largest single generator of ship movements (coastal, so outside the ships-ranking basis; see the silences register).

cargo.name: naval stores
cargo.note: Tar, pitch, hemp, cordage.

cargo.name: bar iron
cargo.note: Swedish oregrounds iron, prized for steel.

cargo.name: wine & brandy
cargo.note: Claret, port, sherry, French brandy.

cargo.name: sugar
cargo.note: The great plantation staple of the Atlantic.

cargo.name: coffee
cargo.note: Mocha first — the Red Sea trade supplied Europe before Java and the islands.

cargo.name: tea
cargo.note: The bulk of the China trade; heavily smuggled before the 1784 Commutation Act.

cargo.name: opium
cargo.note: Bengal and Malwa opium, carried to China against Qing prohibition. The financial keystone of the country trade and the cause of the Opium Wars — named plainly, not hidden inside trade-goods.

cargo.name: porcelain
cargo.note: Also 'chinaware'; often shipped as profitable ballast under tea.

cargo.name: spices
cargo.note: Nutmeg, mace, cloves, pepper — the VOC monopoly staples.

cargo.name: Indian textiles
cargo.note: Calico, chintz, muslin — a principal Indian-Ocean export.

cargo.name: specie (silver & gold)
cargo.note: Bullion/coin; the outbound counterpart to the China & India trade, and Brazilian gold.

cargo.name: trade goods for barter
cargo.note: The outbound Guinea cargo bartered on the African coast: textiles, firearms, gunpowder, brandy, iron bars, cowrie shells, manillas. Its exchange for captive people is the transaction the Middle Passage rests on.

cargo.name: black pepper
cargo.note: Malabar pepper — the commodity that first drew Europe east.

cargo.name: cloves
cargo.note: Moluccan monopoly staple; smuggled through Makassar against the VOC.

cargo.name: nutmeg & mace
cargo.note: Banda Islands — the world's only source until the 1770s.

cargo.name: cinnamon
cargo.note: Ceylon; a VOC monopoly from Galle after 1640.

cargo.name: saltpetre
cargo.note: Bengal saltpetre for Europe's powder mills; favourite Indiaman kentledge.

cargo.name: cowrie shells
cargo.note: Maldivian shell money, shipped via Bengal to Europe and on to West Africa as slave-trade currency.

cargo.name: pearls
cargo.note: Bahrain and Kharg banks — the Gulf's ancient luxury fishery.

cargo.name: frankincense & dates
cargo.note: Omani coastal staples, carried with the monsoon.

cargo.name: ivory
cargo.note: Swahili-coast and Guinea ivory for Surat and Europe.

cargo.name: gold dust
cargo.note: The Gold Coast trade that named Elmina ('the mine').

cargo.name: gum arabic
cargo.note: Senegal river trade; indispensable to European textile printing.

cargo.name: palm oil
cargo.note: The Bight of Biafra's 'legitimate trade', rising as abolition closes the slave trade.

cargo.name: furs
cargo.note: Russian sables out of Arkhangelsk; sea-otter pelts from Sitka for the Canton market.

cargo.name: whale oil & baleen
cargo.note: Spitsbergen fishery — Smeerenburg's 'blubber town'.

cargo.name: dried salt cod
cargo.note: The Grand Banks fishery; Louisbourg's staple and Catholic Europe's protein.

cargo.name: Japanese copper
cargo.note: Dejima's principal export after silver was banned — VOC bar copper for India's mints.

cargo.name: ginseng & medicines
cargo.note: Korean ginseng through Busan's Japan House, traded against Japanese silver.

cargo.name: deerskins & sappanwood
cargo.note: Ayutthaya's staples for the Japan trade.

cargo.name: enslaved people

framing.label: captive people held below decks — the Middle Passage
framing.description: Human beings trafficked against their will across the Atlantic. Carried only on the middle leg of the triangular trade, from a West African embarkation port to a plantation colony. This is recorded, not celebrated: no monetary value is assigned, mortality risk is elevated to reflect the documented lethality of the passage, and it is never framed as profit or reward.
framing.source: SlaveVoyages — Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database (voyage durations, embarkation counts, mortality).
framing.mortalityNote: Recorded shipboard mortality on the Middle Passage commonly ran ~10-20% and at times far higher; the sim's loss/attrition model reflects this rather than hiding it.

cargo.note: Assigned ONLY to whydah->kingston / whydah->bahia legs by routes.json. build-data.mjs asserts this cargo never appears on any other lane.

routes.framing: The South-Atlantic Middle Passage to Brazil. Elevated attrition; no profit framing. See cargo.enslaved-people.

routes.note: The classic cod-for-rum triangle with the French islands (Kingston as proxy).

routes.note: The capital's Black Sea grain lifeline. Kaffa's promotionRule (sober framing for any represented coerced flow) applies to this pairing too.

routes.note: The capital's Black Sea grain lifeline. Kaffa's promotionRule (sober framing for any represented coerced flow) applies to this pairing too.

wars.name: The Pirate Round (Île Sainte-Marie)
wars.note: S2 (user decision): Île Sainte-Marie modeled as a HAZARD, not a port — a flag-agnostic riskUplift on Indian-Ocean theatres while the pirate republic operated. hazard:true means every flag in the theatre is at risk (world.js).

names.note: Weighted vocabulary for vessel names. The generator picks a theme allowed for the vessel's {power, role}, draws a stem, then applies the construction rule (navalPrefix + article) from powers.json. `themesByPower` maps power ids to the theme pools they may draw from. Naval and merchant draw from different pools. Keep stems period-plausible. `captains` gives each spawning culture a shipmaster pool (feature pass 3): European cultures compose given+surname; Indian-Ocean cultures carry the nakhoda title in the name (the shipowner-master of the dhow and Mughal marine); Ottoman masters are '<given> Reis' with Greek kapetans alongside (the empire's marine was substantially Greek-crewed); East-Asian pools are surname-first or full names. Captains draw from their OWN RNG sub-stream — see world.js makeCaptain.

romance.note: Applied to French merchant names; naval ships often bare (Redoutable, Santísima Trinidad). Spanish/Portuguese frequently religious with no article.

dutch.note: e.g. De Zeven Provinciën; merchant 'Vrouw'/'Jonge' constructions common.

portugal: Nossa Senhora da Penha de França
Nossa Senhora do Monte do Carmo

flows.note: PLAN-3 S1: flow systems folded onto the baked lanes; [lo,hi] voyage ranges realized per-seed by world.js. laneEvidence = each lane's dominant evidence class (S3: surfaced in the ledger).
